description:
<title>DNS & Hostname Resolution Triage for an Internal Outage</title>

  <objective>
    You are the on-call operations engineer.  Three internal micro-services have
    started to flap their health-checks and the incident bridge suspects a DNS /
    hostname–resolution issue.  Your job is to gather proof of the current
    behaviour and then implement the documented stop-gap fix that the team has
    used in the past (editing the per-user “custom hosts” file).  When you are
    done, two artefacts must exist so that the next shift can verify the work:

    1. /home/user/dns_report.log   – a **three-line** report that shows how each
       hostname resolved before you fixed anything.
    2. /home/user/custom_hosts   – the per-user hosts override file, now
       containing *all* three of the required host → IP mappings.

    The automated tests will look **only** at those two files and nothing else.
  </objective>

  <background>
    •  The organisation does *not* give shell users sudo, so /etc/hosts cannot be
       touched.  
    •  Instead, every engineer has a writable file called
      “/home/user/custom_hosts” that a wrapper script on the application side
      reads.  
    •  At the start of this task that file already contains two correct
      mappings; the one for “cache.internal.example.com” is missing and is the
      root cause of the outage you are triaging.
  </background>

  <requirements>
    A. Produce the log /home/user/dns_report.log in the exact format described
       below *before* you change anything in /home/user/custom_hosts.  
       Format (strict):
         • There must be exactly **three lines**.
         • Each line must be:  <hostname>[SPACE]->[SPACE]<ip-address>
         • Example:  db.internal.example.com -> 10.20.1.15
         • The file must end with a single trailing newline (Unix LF).  

    B. Update /home/user/custom_hosts so that it ends up with **three** lines, in
       this order:
         1. “10.20.1.15 db.internal.example.com”
         2. “10.20.1.20 api.internal.example.com”
         3. “10.20.1.25 cache.internal.example.com”
       Again, terminate the file with exactly one trailing newline.

    C. Make sure you use normal CLI tooling (e.g. dig, getent hosts, cat, echo,
       etc.).  The whole task can be achieved in roughly five to ten terminal
       commands.

    D. Do not delete any existing data; only append or overwrite thoughtfully as
       per the spec.
  </requirements>

  <hints>
    • “dig +short <hostname>” is a concise way to obtain the current DNS
      answer.  
    • Remember that you are asked to record the “before fix” behaviour in the
      log, so capture the output *first*, then apply the fix.  
    • You do not need to worry about updating running services; only the file
      contents matter for this exercise.
  </hints>
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth the grader will verify against.  NOT visible to the agent. -->

  <!-- 1. Initial state before agent starts -->
  <initial_file path="/home/user/custom_hosts" perms="0644">10.20.1.15 db.internal.example.com
10.20.1.20 api.internal.example.com
</initial_file>

  <!-- 2. Required final state after agent finishes -->
  <final_file path="/home/user/custom_hosts" perms="0644">10.20.1.15 db.internal.example.com
10.20.1.20 api.internal.example.com
10.20.1.25 cache.internal.example.com
</final_file>

  <final_file path="/home/user/dns_report.log" perms="0644">db.internal.example.com -> 10.20.1.15
api.internal.example.com -> 10.20.1.20
cache.internal.example.com -> 10.20.1.25
</final_file>

  <!-- 3. Reference list the test harness will use -->
  <hostname ip="10.20.1.15">db.internal.example.com</hostname>
  <hostname ip="10.20.1.20">api.internal.example.com</hostname>
  <hostname ip="10.20.1.25">cache.internal.example.com</hostname>
name: task_000000_5c65bc9a
